content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 21, 2004

Vanessa Spilios
Brown for Attorney General
409 13th Street, Suite 800
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-214

Dear Ms. Spilios:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the Brown for Attorney General campaign for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May the Brown for Attorney General campaign invest in an auction rate preferred account?

CONCLUSION

No. The auction rate preferred account does not appear to satisfy the requirements of regulation 18524, which allows investment only in certificates of deposit, interest-bearing savings accounts, money market accounts, or similar accounts.

FACTS

The Brown for Attorney General campaign is interested in investing a portion of its monies into an auction rate preferred account. After speaking with a representative at Oppenheimer & Company, you believe this to be a secure investment instrument for the campaign, but seek confirmation before taking further steps.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-214
Page No. 2

You provided information on auction rate investments in the form of the Nuveen
MuniPreferred brochure produced by Nuveen Investments, L.L.C. and a description of
auction rate securities produced by Smith Barney.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires that all contributions to a candidate be deposited into the
candidate's campaign bank account, and that all expenditures be made from this account.
A candidate may transfer funds from the campaign bank account to certificates of
deposit, interest-bearing savings accounts, money market accounts, or similar accounts.
These funds must be redeposited in the campaign bank account before expenditure.
(Regulation 18524(b).)

The Commission has authorized the transfer of campaign funds to interest-bearing
savings accounts held in financial institutions. (Ayala Advice Letter, No. A-95-017.) The
Commission has advised that such investment accounts may be maintained only at state
and national banks, state and federal savings and loan associations, state credit unions,
and federal credit unions located in the state. (Bauer Advice Letter, No. I-91-181.)

Auction rate securities are described in the materials you provided and in the
CDIAC brief as stocks backed by municipal bonds (Nuveen brochure).² Neither stocks
nor bonds fit within the exception to the bank account rules articulated in regulation
18524 and prior Commission advice. You do not indicate that the investment account
would be held in an appropriate financial institution. For these reasons, an investment in
auction rate securities is not a "similar account" comparable to certificates of deposit,
interest-bearing savings accounts, or money market accounts permissible under
regulation 18524.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Hal Dasinger
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure
HD:LM:jg
J\Uploads\04214.doc

² Also, a description of auction rate securities issued by Douglas Skarr, "Auction Rate Securities,"
California Debt and Investment Advisory Commission, August 2004 (Issue Brief) describes these securities
as long-term, variable-rate bonds tied to short-term interest rates.
